title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: vignette demonstrates Partial Credit Model (PCM) Rasch analysis workflow using easyRaschBayes. analysis uses eRm::pcmdat2 dataset, small polytomous item response data set included eRm package. functions package work Bayesian brms model object fitted acat (adjacent categories) family, parameterises PCM. Dichotomous Rasch models can also fit using brms analyzed functions package. code example available , detail available Bürkner, 2021. , brief texts explaining output interpretation key functions. extensive treatment various Rasch analysis aspects, please see easyRasch vignette. Also, function includes documentation example code, use ?function console.

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: eRm::pcmdat2 wide format item responses coded 0, 1, 2, …. brms acat family expects response categories starting 1, add 1 every response reshaping long format.
code: library(easyRaschBayes) library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble) library(ggplot2) df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  head(df_pcm) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   id    item  response #>   <chr> <chr>    <dbl> #> 1 1     I1           2 #> 2 1     I2           2 #> 3 1     I3           2 #> 4 1     I4           2 #> 5 2     I1           1 #> 6 2     I2           1

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: model fitted saved disk. code chunk shows fitting call (evaluated R CMD check). pre-fitted model stored fits/fit_pcm.rds loaded instead.
code: prior_pcm <- prior("normal(0, 3)", class = "Intercept") +   prior("normal(0, 3)", class = "sd", group = "id") fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data    = df_pcm,   family  = acat,   prior   = prior_pcm,   chains  = 4,   cores   = 4,   iter    = 2000 ) saveRDS(fit_pcm, "fits/fit_pcm.rds") fit_pcm <- readRDS("fits/fit_pcm.rds")

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: infit_statistic() computes posterior predictive infit outfit statistics item. Values near 1.0 indicate good fit; values substantially 1 suggest underfit (unexpected responses), values 1 suggest overfit (predictable). ndraws_use argument limits number posterior draws used, speeds computation exploration. final reporting, use draws (set ndraws_use = NULL omit ). plot chunk infit infit_obs indicates observed conditional infit, can compared infit_rep, akin model expected value. Posterior predictive p-values (*_ppp) close 0.5 indicate observed statistic falls near centre posterior predictive distribution, implying good fit. Values near 0 1 warrant investigation.
code: fit_stats <- infit_statistic(fit_pcm, ndraws_use = 500)  # Post-process Infit infit_results <- infit_post(fit_stats) infit_results$summary #> # A tibble: 4 × 4 #>   item  infit_obs infit_rep infit_ppp #>   <chr>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 I1        1.04      1.00      0.304 #> 2 I2        1.08      1.00      0.14  #> 3 I3        0.918     0.999     0.866 #> 4 I4        1.03      0.997     0.31 infit_results$plot

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: item_restscore_statistic() computes Goodman-Kruskal’s gamma item’s observed responses rest score (total score minus focal item). well-fitting Rasch model, gamma positive moderate; high values may indicate redundancy, low negative values suggest item relate well latent trait. plot chunk restscore
code: rest_stats <- item_restscore_statistic(fit_pcm, ndraws_use = 500)  rest_results <- item_restscore_post(rest_stats) rest_results$summary #> # A tibble: 4 × 5 #>   item  gamma_obs gamma_rep gamma_diff   ppp #>   <chr>     <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 I1        0.473     0.541     -0.068 0.108 #> 2 I2        0.441     0.548     -0.107 0.042 #> 3 I3        0.643     0.536      0.107 0.966 #> 4 I4        0.535     0.54      -0.005 0.48 rest_results$plot

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: plot chunk ciccplot
code: plot_icc(fit_pcm)

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: plot_residual_pca() performs principal components analysis person-item residuals plots standardized loadings first residual contrast factor together item locations uncertainty . plot chunk pca-plot Items positive loadings cluster one end, negative loadings . observed largest eigenvalue smaller replicated, unidimensionality supported. ppp close 1.
code: pca <- plot_residual_pca(fit_pcm, ndraws_use = 500) pca$plot

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: q3_statistic() computes Yen’s Q3 statistic — correlation person-item residuals every item pair. conditioning latent trait, residuals uncorrelated; elevated Q3 values indicate local dependence (LD). primary metric ppp, close 1. output filtered ppp values 0.95. plot chunk q3
code: q3_stats <- q3_statistic(fit_pcm, ndraws_use = 500)  q3_results <- q3_post(q3_stats) q3_results$summary #> # A tibble: 6 × 7 #>   item_pair item_1 item_2 q3_obs q3_rep q3_diff q3_ppp #>   <chr>     <chr>  <chr>   <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1 I3 : I4   I3     I4      0.342  0.002   0.34   1     #> 2 I1 : I2   I1     I2      0.103 -0.001   0.104  0.998 #> 3 I1 : I3   I1     I3     -0.069  0      -0.069  0.016 #> 4 I2 : I3   I2     I3     -0.086 -0.004  -0.082  0.008 #> 5 I1 : I4   I1     I4     -0.131  0.003  -0.134  0     #> 6 I2 : I4   I2     I4     -0.163 -0.003  -0.16   0 q3_results$plot

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: plot shows probability using response category y axis latent score x axis. crossover points, lines meet, item category threshold locations. Uncertainty shown shaded area around line. plot chunk ipf-plot
code: plot_ipf(fit_pcm, theta_range = c(-6,5))

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: plot_targeting() produces Wright map (person–item targeting plot) showing distribution person locations alongside item threshold locations logit scale. Good targeting occurs person item distributions overlap substantially. plot chunk targeting
code: plot_targeting(fit_pcm)

title: Partial Credit Model Analysis with easyRaschBayes
text: RMUreliability() provides Bayesian reliability estimate via Relative Measurement Uncertainty (RMU, see Bignardi et al., 2025). requires matrix person location draws dimensions persons×drawspersons × draws. output point estimate lower/upper 95% highest density continuous intervals (HDCI). RMU values range 0 1, higher values indicating higher reliability, similarly traditional reliability metrics Cronbach’s alpha.
code: person_draws <- fit_pcm %>%   as_draws_df() %>%   as_tibble() %>%    select(starts_with("r_id")) %>%   t() rmu <- RMUreliability(person_draws) rmu #>   rmu_estimate hdci_lowerbound hdci_upperbound .width .point .interval #> 1    0.6723774       0.6167611       0.7258928   0.95   mean      hdci

code: ipar <- item_parameters(fit_pcm) knitr::kable(ipar$summary) knitr::kable(ipar$locations_wide)

code: ppar <- person_parameters(fit_pcm) knitr::kable(ppar$score_table) hist(ppar$person_estimates$eap, col = "lightblue", main = "Histogram of EAP scores")

title: Bayesian Rasch Analysis Using brms
text: easyRaschBayes provides functions reproduce classic Rasch analysis features using Bayesian item response theory (IRT) models fitted brms. supports dichotomous Rasch models polytomous partial credit models, exposes full posterior distribution output. materials Rasch analysis, see vignette (frequentist) package easyRasch.

title: Bayesian Rasch Analysis Using brms
text: Install stable version CRAN: install development version GitHub:
code: install.packages("easyRaschBayes") install.packages("remotes") # if you don't have `remotes` installed remotes::install_github("pgmj/easyRaschBayes")

title: Bayesian Rasch Analysis Using brms
code: library(brms) library(easyRaschBayes)  # Fit a Bayesian Rasch model (dichotomous) fit <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = my_data,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4, cores = 4 )  # Conditional item infit infit <- infit_statistic(fit) infit_post(infit)  # Local dependence with Yen's Q3 q3_results <- q3_statistic(fit) q3_post(q3_results)  # Response category functioning plot_ipf(fit)  # Person-item map plot_targeting(fit)

text: input_draws matrix data frame posterior draws. Rows represent subjects columns represent draws. verbose Logical. Print detailed information input data. Default TRUE. level Numeric. Credibility level highest density continuous interval. Default 0.95.

text: list containing: hdci: data frame point-estimate (posterior mean) highest density continuous interval reliability, calculated using ggdist::mean_hdci function reliability_posterior_draws: numeric vector posterior draws reliability, length K/2 (K = number columns/draws input_draws matrix)

code: # \donttest{ # See https://www.bignardi.co.uk/8_bayes_reliability/tutorial_rmu_sum_score_reliability.html # for more details on this example  # Simulate data  library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union library(tidyr) library(brms) #> Loading required package: Rcpp #> Loading 'brms' package (version 2.23.0). Useful instructions #> can be found by typing help('brms'). A more detailed introduction #> to the package is available through vignette('brms_overview'). #>  #> Attaching package: ‘brms’ #> The following object is masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     ar set.seed(1) N                   = 5000 # number of subjects (mice) J                   = 3    # number of measurements per subject true_score_variance = 1 error_variance      = 10  df = expand.grid(j = 1:J, mouse = 1:N)  true_scores       = rnorm(N, mean = 10, sd = sqrt(true_score_variance)) measurement_error = rnorm(N*J, mean = 0, sd = sqrt(error_variance))  df$measurement = true_scores[df$mouse] + measurement_error  df_average_lengths = df %>%   group_by(mouse) %>%   summarise(average_measurement = mean(measurement))  # Reliability should equal this:  true_score_variance/(true_score_variance+error_variance/J) #> [1] 0.2307692  # Approximately the same as:  cor(df_average_lengths$average_measurement, true_scores)^2 #> [1] 0.2414736  # Fit model and calculate RMU  brms_model = brm(   measurement ~ 1 + (1 | mouse),   data    = df,    iter = 500,   warmup = 150 ) #> Compiling Stan program... #> Error in .fun(model_code = .x1): Boost not found; call install.packages('BH')  # Extract posterior draws from brms model  posterior_draws = brms_model %>%   as_draws_df() %>%   as_tibble %>%    select(starts_with("r_mouse")) %>%   t() #> Error: object 'brms_model' not found  # Calculate RMU  RMUreliability(posterior_draws)$hdci #> Error: object 'posterior_draws' not found # }

title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
text: model fitted brmsfit object baseline (-DIF) model. group_var unquoted variable name identifying grouping variable DIF testing (e.g., gender). Must factor character variable exactly 2 levels current implementation. item_var unquoted variable name identifying item grouping variable. Default item. person_var unquoted variable name identifying person grouping variable. Default id. data optional data frame containing variables needed DIF model, including group variable. NULL (default), function attempts use model$data. Since baseline model formula typically include group variable, brms dropped stored model data. case, must supply original data frame . dif_type Character. polytomous ordinal models . "uniform" (default) tests uniform location shift per item via group:item fixed-effect interaction. "non-uniform" fits group-specific thresholds per item computes per-threshold DIF effects difference groups. Ignored dichotomous models. prob Numeric \((0, 1)\). Width credible intervals. Default 0.95. rope Numeric. Half-width Region Practical Equivalence (ROPE) around zero DIF effects, logit scale. Default 0.5, corresponding practically negligible DIF effect. Set 0 skip ROPE analysis. refit Logical. TRUE (default), DIF model fitted automatically updating baseline model via update, reuses compiled Stan code faster sampling. FALSE, DIF model formula returned (useful manual fitting custom settings). ... Additional arguments passed update.brmsfit refitting DIF model (e.g., cores, control).

title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
text: list following elements: summary tibble one row per item (uniform DIF) per item × threshold (non-uniform DIF) containing: item, optionally threshold, dif_estimate (posterior mean), dif_lower, dif_upper (credible interval), dif_sd (posterior SD), pd (probability direction), rope_percentage (proportion inside ROPE), flag (classification). dif_draws matrix posterior draws DIF effects (draws × effects), analysis. dif_model fitted DIF brmsfit object (refit = TRUE), NULL. dif_formula brmsformula used DIF model. baseline_model original baseline model. plot ggplot forest plot DIF effects credible intervals ROPE.

title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
text: polytomous models, two types DIF can tested: Uniform DIF (dif_type = "uniform", default) single location shift per item across groups, modelled group:item fixed-effect interaction. tests whether average item difficulty differs groups. Non-uniform / threshold-level DIF (dif_type = "non-uniform") item receives group-specific thresholds via thres(gr = interaction(item, group)). DIF effects computed difference threshold groups, revealing whether DIF affects specific response categories. function constructs DIF model adding group--item interaction baseline model: Dichotomous models (family = bernoulli()): baseline response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id) becomes response ~ 1 + group + (1 + group | item) + (1 | id), group slope varying item captures item-specific DIF. Polytomous uniform DIF (dif_type = "uniform"): baseline response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id) becomes response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + group:item + (1 | id). Polytomous non-uniform DIF (dif_type = "non-uniform"): baseline becomes response | thres(gr = item_group) ~ 1 + (1 | id), item_group = interaction(item, group). item × group combination gets thresholds. DIF effects differences group-specific thresholds item, computed draw--draw posterior. DIF effects summarized using: Probability Direction (pd) proportion posterior dominant side zero. Values > 0.975 indicate strong directional evidence. ROPE Region Practical Equivalence (Kruschke, 2018). > 95\ DIF effect practically negligible. > 95\ outside, effect practically significant. Credible Interval CI excludes zero, evidence DIF specified credibility level.

title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic

title: Differential Item Functioning (DIF) Analysis for Bayesian IRT Models — dif_statistic
code: # \donttest{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Dichotomous Rasch with DIF testing ---  set.seed(123) df <- expand.grid(id = 1:200, item = paste0("I", 1:10)) %>%   mutate(     gender = rep(sample(c("M", "F"), 200, TRUE), each = 10),     theta  = rep(rnorm(200), each = 10),     delta  = rep(seq(-2, 2, length.out = 10), 200),     dif    = ifelse(item == "I3" & gender == "F", 1.0,              ifelse(item == "I7" & gender == "F", -0.8, 0)),     p      = plogis(theta - delta - dif),     response = rbinom(n(), 1, p)   )  fit_base <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = df,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,    cores  = 2, # use more cores if you have   iter   = 1000 # use at least 2000  ) #> Compiling Stan program... #> Error in .fun(model_code = .x1): Boost not found; call install.packages('BH')  dif_result <- dif_statistic(   model     = fit_base,   group_var = gender,   data      = df ) #> Error: object 'fit_base' not found  dif_result$summary #> Error: object 'dif_result' not found dif_result$plot #> Error: object 'dif_result' not found  # --- Partial Credit Model: uniform DIF ---  df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   mutate(gender = sample(c("M", "F"), n(), TRUE)) %>%   pivot_longer(!c(id, gender),                names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data   = df_pcm,   family = acat,   chains = 4,    cores  = 2, # use more cores if you have   iter   = 1000 # use at least 2000  ) #> Compiling Stan program... #> Error in .fun(model_code = .x1): Boost not found; call install.packages('BH')  # Uniform DIF (default): one shift per item dif_uni <- dif_statistic(fit_pcm, group_var = gender, data = df_pcm) #> Error: object 'fit_pcm' not found dif_uni$plot #> Error: object 'dif_uni' not found  # Non-uniform DIF: threshold-level effects dif_nu <- dif_statistic(fit_pcm, group_var = gender, data = df_pcm,                          dif_type = "non-uniform") #> Error: object 'fit_pcm' not found dif_nu$summary #> Error: object 'dif_nu' not found dif_nu$plot #> Error: object 'dif_nu' not found # }

title: Posterior Predictive Item Fit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — fit_statistic_pcm
text: function implements posterior predictive checking approach item fit described Bürkner (2020). procedure works follows: Draw posterior expected category probabilities via posterior_epred posterior predicted responses via posterior_predict. ordinal categorical models (3D array output posterior_epred), extract probability assigned observed response category replicated response category draw observation. Apply user-supplied criterion function compute pointwise fit values observed replicated data. Aggregate (sum) criterion values within level group posterior draw. common choice ordinal IRT models categorical log-likelihood criterion function(y, p) log(p). binary (e.g., dichotomous Rasch) models, Bernoulli log-likelihood function(y, p) y * log(p) + (1 - y) * log(1 - p) may used instead.

title: Posterior Predictive Item Fit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — fit_statistic_pcm
code: # \donttest{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Polytomous Rasch (Partial Credit Model) ---  # Prepare data in long format df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  # Fit a Partial Credit Model using the adjacent category family fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data    = df_pcm,   family  = acat,   chains  = 4,   cores   = 1, # use more cores if you have   iter    = 500 # use at least 2000  ) #> Compiling Stan program... #> Error in .fun(model_code = .x1): Boost not found; call install.packages('BH')  # Categorical log-likelihood criterion (for polytomous models) ll_categorical <- function(y, p) log(p)  # Compute item fit statistics item_fit <- fit_statistic_pcm(   model      = fit_pcm,   criterion  = ll_categorical,   group      = item,   ndraws_use = 100 # use at least 500 ) #> Error: object 'fit_pcm' not found  # Summarise: posterior predictive p-values per item item_fit %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   ) #> Error: object 'item_fit' not found  # Use ggplot2 to make a histogram library(ggplot2) item_fit %>%   ggplot(aes(crit_diff)) +   geom_histogram(aes(fill = ifelse(crit_diff > 0, "above","below"))) +   facet_wrap("item") +   theme_bw() +   theme(legend.position = "none") #> Error: object 'item_fit' not found  # Compute person fit statistics person_fit <- fit_statistic_pcm(   model      = fit_pcm,   criterion  = ll_categorical,   group      = id,   ndraws_use = 100 # use at least 500 ) #> Error: object 'fit_pcm' not found # }

code: # \donttest{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Dichotomous Rasch Model ---  # Prepare binary response data in long format df_rm <- eRm::raschdat3 %>%   as.data.frame() %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  # Fit a dichotomous Rasch model fit_rm <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores   = 1, # use more cores if you have   iter    = 500 # use at least 2000  ) #> Compiling Stan program... #> Error in .fun(model_code = .x1): Boost not found; call install.packages('BH')  # Bernoulli log-likelihood criterion ll_bernoulli <- function(y, p) y * log(p) + (1 - y) * log(1 - p)  # Compute item fit statistics item_fit <- fit_statistic_rm(   model      = fit_rm,   criterion  = ll_bernoulli,   group      = item,   ndraws_use = 100 # use at least 500 ) #> Error: object 'fit_rm' not found  # Summarise: posterior predictive p-values per item item_fit %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   ) #> Error: object 'item_fit' not found  # Use ggplot2 to make a histogram library(ggplot2) item_fit %>%   ggplot(aes(crit_diff)) +   geom_histogram(aes(fill = ifelse(crit_diff > 0, "above","below"))) +   facet_wrap("item") +   theme_bw() +   theme(legend.position = "none") #> Error: object 'item_fit' not found  # Compute person fit statistics person_fit <- fit_statistic_rm(   model      = fit_rm,   criterion  = ll_bernoulli,   group      = id,   ndraws_use = 100 # use at least 500 ) #> Error: object 'fit_rm' not found  person_fit %>%   group_by(id) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   ) #> Error: object 'person_fit' not found  # --- 1PL model with item-specific intercepts ---  # Alternative parameterisation with fixed item effects fit_1pl <- brm(   response ~ 0 + item + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores   = 1, # use more cores if you have   iter    = 500 # use at least 2000  ) #> Compiling Stan program... #> Error in .fun(model_code = .x1): Boost not found; call install.packages('BH')  item_fit_1pl <- fit_statistic_rm(   model      = fit_1pl,   criterion  = ll_bernoulli,   group      = item,   ndraws_use = 100 # use at least 500 ) #> Error: object 'fit_1pl' not found  item_fit_1pl %>%   group_by(item) %>%   summarise(     observed   = mean(crit),     replicated = mean(crit_rep),     ppp        = mean(crit_rep > crit)   ) #> Error: object 'item_fit_1pl' not found # }

title: Summarize and Plot Posterior Predictive Infit Statistics — infit_post
text: Two complementary summary tables provided: summary Reports posterior mean observed replicated infit values alongside posterior predictive p-value (ppp). ppp proportion draws replicated infit exceeds observed infit. good fit, ppp near 0.5. ppp near 0 indicates observed infit consistently larger expected (underfit); ppp near 1 indicates consistently smaller (overfit). hdi Reports probability observed infit falls (underfit) (overfit) HDI replicated distribution. provides distributional assessment ppp alone. plot uses two layers ggdist package: stat_slab Displays posterior predictive (replicated) infit distribution filled density slab per item, shaded credible interval level. stat_slabinterval Displays observed infit distribution per item semi-transparent slab point interval summaries. good model fit, observed infit distribution overlap substantially replicated distribution. Items observed distribution sits systematically replicated HDI indicate underfit (variation expected); items indicate overfit (less variation expected).

Posterior predictive p-values can obtained computing, e.g., mean(infit_rep > infit) within item.

title: Posterior Predictive Infit Statistic for Bayesian IRT Models — infit_statistic
text: procedure adapts conditional infit/outfit statistics (Christensen et al., 2013; Kreiner & Christensen, 2011; Müller, 2020) Bayesian framework: posterior draw \(s\), category probabilities \(P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)\) obtained posterior_epred. conditional expected value variance observation computed : $$E^{(s)}_{vi} = \sum_c c \cdot P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)$$ $$Var^{(s)}_{vi} = \sum_c (c - E^{(s)}_{vi})^2 \cdot       P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)$$ Standardised squared residuals : $$Z^{2(s)}_{vi} = (X_{vi} - E^{(s)}_{vi})^2 / Var^{(s)}_{vi}$$ infit statistic item \(\) variance-weighted mean \(Z^2\) across persons: $$Infit_i^{(s)} = \frac{\sum_v Var_{vi}^{(s)} Z^{2(s)}_{vi}}       {\sum_v Var_{vi}^{(s)}}$$ requested, outfit unweighted mean \(Z^2\).

title: Extract Item Parameters from a Bayesian Rasch Model — item_parameters
text: Dichotomous models random item effects (response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id)) parameterise item difficulty \(\delta_i = -(b_0 + r_i)\) \(b_0\) global intercept \(r_i\) item random effect. Models fixed item effects (response ~ 0 + item + (1 | id)) parameterise difficulty \(\delta_i = -b_i\). Polytomous (acat/PCM) models grouped thresholds (thres(gr = item)) directly estimate item-specific threshold parameters. row long output represents one threshold within one item; row wide output represents one item. Item information computed posterior mean item parameters using standard Rasch/PCM information formulae: Dichotomous \(I_i(\theta) = P_i(\theta) Q_i(\theta)\) \(P_i = \text{logistic}(\theta - \delta_i)\). Polytomous (PCM) \(I_i(\theta) = \sum_c (c - E_i)^2 P_{ic}(\theta)\) \(E_i = \sum_c c \cdot P_{ic}\) expected score item \(\). Threshold ordering: partial credit model, disordered thresholds (\(\tau_{k+1} \le \tau_k\)) indicate probability responding intermediate category never exceeds adjacent categories — category empirically "absorbed". necessarily indicate misfit (see Adams et al., 2012), may suggest response categories collapsed. prob_disordered column reports posterior probability least one disordered pair per item, providing Bayesian alternative post-hoc threshold checks.

title: Posterior Predictive Item-Restscore Association for Bayesian IRT Models — item_restscore_statistic
text: item-restscore association key diagnostic Rasch measurement. Rasch model, item relate latent trait (hence rest-score) modelled relationship. Goodman-Kruskal's gamma rank-based measure association ordinal cross-tabulations well-suited purpose (Kreiner, 2011). procedure posterior draw \(s\) : Obtain replicated responses \(Y^{rep(s)}\) posterior_predict. item \(\) person \(v\), compute rest-score: \(R^{obs}_{vi} = \sum_{j \neq } X_{vj}\) observed data \(R^{rep(s)}_{vi} = \sum_{j \neq }     Y^{rep(s)}_{vj}\) replicated data. Cross-tabulate item score \(\times\) rest-score compute Goodman-Kruskal gamma observed replicated data. Compare two gammas across draws. Items ppp close 1 observed item-restscore association consistently stronger model predicts. typically indicates item discriminates assumed equal-discrimination Rasch model (.e., violation Rasch assumption). Items ppp close 0 discriminate less expected.

title: Extract Person Parameters from a Bayesian Rasch Model — person_parameters
text: EAP estimates extracted posterior means person random effects (r_id[j, Intercept]) as_draws_df, posterior SD serving standard error. standard Bayesian point estimates reflect shrinkage toward population mean. WLE estimates (Warm, 1989) computed posterior mean item parameters using Newton-Raphson iteration adaptive step damping Warm-corrected likelihood. WLE adds bias correction term \(J(\theta) / (2 (\theta))\) score equations, \((\theta)\) test information \(J(\theta) = \sum_i \sum_c (c - E_i)^3 P_{ic}\) sum third central moments. produces estimates reduced finite-sample bias compared MLE, especially extreme scores (Warm, 1989). Newton-Raphson algorithm uses adaptive step damping following approach iarm (Mueller): maximum allowed step size shrinks factor 1.05 iteration, preventing overshoot ensuring convergence near-extreme scores. extreme scores (sum score = 0 maximum possible), WLE well-defined. cases assigned boundary values theta_range NA standard errors. center = TRUE (default), item difficulty parameters shifted mean zero, EAP person parameters shifted constant. WLE computed centered item parameters. matches convention frequentist CML Rasch estimation. Row ordering: person_estimates draws_matrix preserve original person order model data (order first appearance person ID). allows direct row-binding source data without re-matching.

title: Item Characteristic Curves with Class Intervals — plot_icc
text: Bayesian analogue ICCplot() iarm package, using class interval method. Expected curve: item, expected item score \(E_i(\theta)\) computed dense grid theta values using posterior draws item thresholds. adjacent category (PCM/acat) family: $$E_i(\theta) = \sum_{c=0}^{K} c \cdot P_{ic}(\theta)$$ \(P_{ic}\) category probabilities. dichotomous items, \(E_i(\theta) = P_i(\theta)\). posterior mean \(E_i(\theta)\) plotted line, credible interval ribbon showing prob interval across posterior draws. Class intervals: Following approach iarm::ICCplot(), persons binned class intervals ordinal sum score (sufficient statistic Rasch models), EAP theta estimate. Within interval, mean observed item response computed plotted theta value corresponding mean sum score interval, obtained inverting posterior-mean expected total score function. Persons extreme sum scores (0 maximum) excluded class intervals, theta positions estimated. Uncertainty around observed points: class interval, standard error mean observed response computed displayed error bars showing \(\pm 1\) SE. reflect sampling variability observed mean within bin. DIF overlay: dif_var specified, observed scores computed separately level DIF variable, producing group-specific points connected lines. Deviations groups track alongside (offset expected curve) suggest uniform DIF. Crossing group lines suggest non-uniform DIF. Partial gamma: dif_stats = TRUE (default DIF requested), Goodman-Kruskal partial gamma coefficient displayed item panel. measures ordinal association item response DIF group, stratified total score. title: Residual PCA Contrast Plot for Bayesian IRT Models — plot_residual_pca
text: procedure posterior draw \(s\) : Obtain category probabilities posterior_epred. Compute expected values \(E^{(s)}_{vi}\) variances \(Var^{(s)}_{vi}\). Compute standardized residuals observed data: $$z^{obs(s)}_{vi} = \frac{X_{vi} - E^{(s)}_{vi}}       {\sqrt{Var^{(s)}_{vi}}}$$ Generate replicated data \(Y^{rep(s)}\) posterior_predict compute standardized residuals: $$z^{rep(s)}_{vi} = \frac{Y^{rep(s)}_{vi} -       E^{(s)}_{vi}}{\sqrt{Var^{(s)}_{vi}}}$$ Reshape sets residuals person \(\times\) item matrices perform SVD . Extract first-contrast eigenvalue item loadings observed replicated SVDs. Compare eigenvalues across draws: posterior predictive p-value ppp = mean(eigenvalue_obs > eigenvalue_rep) tests whether observed residual structure stronger model produces assumptions. style = "density" style = "", draw-level (location, loading) pairs item used construct filled 2D kernel density contours via geom_density_2d_filled. lowest contour level (outside contours) set transparent white panel background shows . Higher density regions use progressively darker fills. Item loadings aligned across draws using majority-sign alignment resolve sign indeterminacy eigenvectors.

text: model fitted brmsfit object. Supported parameterisations: Polytomous ordinal e.g., family = acat thres(gr = item), producing item-specific thresholds. Dichotomous Rasch (random items) e.g., response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id) family = bernoulli(). Dichotomous 1PL (fixed items) e.g., response ~ 0 + item + (1 | id) family = bernoulli(). item_var unquoted variable name identifying item grouping variable model data. Default item. person_var unquoted variable name identifying person grouping variable model data. Default id. mult Numeric multiplier applied posterior SD used prior SD. Values > 1 widen priors (less informative); values < 1 tighten . Default 1 (use posterior SD directly).

title: Extract Informative Priors from a Fitted Bayesian IRT Model — posterior_to_prior
text: brmsprior object can supplied prior argument brm update.

title: Extract Informative Priors from a Fitted Bayesian IRT Model — posterior_to_prior
text: function extracts posterior draws via as_draws_df, computes mean SD parameter's marginal posterior, constructs normal(mean, sd * mult) priors. Polytomous ordinal models grouped thresholds (thres(gr = item)): threshold receives prior via brms::set_prior("normal(...)", class = "Intercept", group = item, coef = threshold_index). Dichotomous Rasch models parameterised response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id): priors set global intercept (class = "Intercept"), item-level SD (class = "sd", group = item_var), person-level SD. Dichotomous 1PL models parameterised response ~ 0 + item + (1 | id): item-specific fixed effect (e.g., b_itemI1) receives normal(mean, sd) prior via brms::set_prior(..., class = "b", coef = "itemI1"). cases person-level SD receives normal(mean, sd * mult) prior (brms applies lower bound zero automatically SD parameters).

title: Posterior Predictive Q3 Residual Correlations for Bayesian IRT Models — q3_statistic
text: procedure works follows posterior draw \(s\): Compute expected values \(E^{(s)}_{vi}\) category probabilities returned posterior_epred. ordinal models: \(E^{(s)}_{vi} = \sum_c c \cdot     P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = c)\). binary models: \(E^{(s)}_{vi} =     P^{(s)}(X_{vi} = 1)\). Compute observed residuals: \(d^{(s)}_{vi} = X_{vi} -     E^{(s)}_{vi}\). Compute replicated residuals: \(d^{rep(s)}_{vi} =     Y^{rep(s)}_{vi} - E^{(s)}_{vi}\), \(Y^{rep}\) drawn via posterior_predict. item pair \((, j)\), compute Q3 Pearson correlation residuals across persons responded items.

code: # \donttest{ library(brms) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(tibble)  # --- Partial Credit Model ---  df_pcm <- eRm::pcmdat2 %>%   mutate(across(everything(), ~ .x + 1)) %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_pcm <- brm(   response | thres(gr = item) ~ 1 + (1 | id),   data   = df_pcm,   family = acat,   chains = 4,   cores  = 1, # use more cores if you have   iter   = 500 # use at least 2000  ) #> Compiling Stan program... #> Error in .fun(model_code = .x1): Boost not found; call install.packages('BH')  # Q3 residual correlations q3_draws <- q3_statistic(fit_pcm, ndraws_use = 500) #> Error: object 'fit_pcm' not found  # Postprocess result <- q3_post(q3_draws) #> Error: object 'q3_draws' not found result$summary #> Error: object 'result' not found result$hdi #> Error: object 'result' not found result$plot #> Error: object 'result' not found  # --- Dichotomous Rasch Model ---  df_rm <- eRm::raschdat3 %>%   as.data.frame() %>%   rownames_to_column("id") %>%   pivot_longer(!id, names_to = "item", values_to = "response")  fit_rm <- brm(   response ~ 1 + (1 | item) + (1 | id),   data   = df_rm,   family = bernoulli(),   chains = 4,   cores  = 1, # use more cores if you have   iter   = 500 # use at least 2000  ) #> Compiling Stan program... #> Error in .fun(model_code = .x1): Boost not found; call install.packages('BH')  q3_draws <- q3_statistic(fit_rm, ndraws_use = 500) #> Error: object 'fit_rm' not found  # Postprocess result <- q3_post(q3_draws) #> Error: object 'q3_draws' not found result$summary #> Error: object 'result' not found result$hdi #> Error: object 'result' not found result$plot #> Error: object 'result' not found # }

title: easyRaschBayes 0.2.0 (development version)
text: infit_statistic() now faster defaults output outfit statistics, since dubious value (see Müller 2020 Johansson, 2025). item_restscore_statistic() now faster output similar infit_post(). q3_statistic() now faster output similar infit_post(). plot_residual_pca() now faster. infit_post() output infit_statistic() item_restscore_post() output item_restscore_statistic() q3_post() output q3_statistic() plot_icc() can produce basic conditional ICC plot optionally DIF ICC plot, latter also reporting partial gamma DIF magnitude coefficient. inspired iarm::ICCplot() item_parameters() retrieve item threshold locations person_parameters() retrieve person locations (latent scores) using EAP WLE, also transformation table ordinal sum score EAP/WLE. posterior_to_prior() extracts priors brmsfit model may useful model fit assessment. article added later, exploring .
